1: The theme: "a good name is better than fine perfume, and the day of death than the day of birth." The preference for the day of death over the day of birth — counterintuitive wisdom — anticipates the NT's valuation of death transformed by resurrection: Paul echoes this when he says "to die is gain" (Philippians 1:21). Jesus' name, poured out like perfume (Song of Solomon 1:3), is the name above every name, the good name that cannot be taken away (Philippians 2:9).
2: The theme: it is better to go to the house of mourning than the house of feasting, for death is the end of every person and the living should take it to heart. Jesus goes to both: he weeps at Lazarus' grave (John 11:35) and feasts with sinners (Luke 15:2). His Beatitudes — "Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted" (Matthew 5:4) — take up this Ecclesiastes commendation of the house of mourning and add the resurrection comfort Qohelet could not yet see.
3: The theme: sorrow is better than laughter, for a sad face does the heart good. The wisdom that suffering produces depth rather than shallowness runs through the Psalms of lament, the Servant Songs, and Hebrews: Jesus is "acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3) and "learned obedience through what he suffered" (Hebrews 5:8). The NT does not abolish this wisdom but anchors it in the one whose sorrow was the world's healing.
4: The theme: the wise heart is drawn to the house of mourning, the fool's to the house of mirth. Wisdom that refuses to evade grief is the posture of the prophets and, ultimately, of Christ, who does not avoid Jerusalem and the cross but sets his face toward them (Luke 9:51). The Wisdom of God takes the path of mourning willingly.
5: The revelation of God: it is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than the song of fools — a Wisdom principle that recurs in Proverbs (Proverbs 27:6). Christ is the wise teacher whose rebuke — "woe to you" — is better for those who receive it than any flattery; the prophetic tradition of hard truth-telling climaxes in him who speaks the word that judges and saves (Hebrews 4:12).
6: The theme: the crackling of thorns under a pot — the fool's laughter — bright and quick and fruitless. This image of ephemeral noise has its NT counterpart in Paul's "noisy gong or clanging cymbal" (1 Corinthians 13:1) — the person who has gifts but not love. Love, which Christ embodies and commands, is the substance that outlasts the noise.
7: The revelation of God: oppression makes a fool of a wise man; a bribe corrupts the heart — the social forces that undo wisdom. Christ's own trial is the extreme case: oppression and bribes (the thirty pieces of silver) corrupt the judgment of the powerful against the Wise One. His cross is the point where human corruption reaches its nadir and divine wisdom turns it to redemption (1 Corinthians 1:25).
8: The theme: the end of a matter is better than its beginning; patience of spirit is better than pride. Paul's "patient endurance" and Hebrews's "endure to the end" draw on this wisdom principle. Christ himself endured to the end, and the letter to the Hebrews presents his completion — "It is finished" (John 19:30) — as the end-is-better-than-beginning principle taken to its ultimate expression.
9: The theme: do not be hasty to become angry, for anger rests in the lap of fools. Christ embodies this in his "slow to anger" character (Exodus 34:6 describes the God of whom he is the full expression), and James picks up the Ecclesiastes wisdom directly: "Be slow to speak and slow to become angry" (James 1:19). The one who could have called twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) chose patience instead.
10: The theme: "do not say, 'Why were the former days better than these?'" — the wisdom against nostalgic retreat. The NT does not commend nostalgia for the old covenant but forward movement into the new: Christ is the new thing God is doing (2 Corinthians 5:17), and the one who looks back after putting his hand to the plow is not fit for the kingdom (Luke 9:62).
11: The theme: wisdom like an inheritance is a good thing, benefiting those who see the sun. In the NT, the inheritance is not wisdom as an abstract quality but "the wisdom of God" who is Christ (1 Corinthians 1:24), the inheritance secured by the Spirit as down payment (Ephesians 1:14). The "seeing the sun" language resonates with the Johannine light motif: Christ as the light of the world (John 8:12).
12: The theme: wisdom and money both protect, but wisdom "preserves the life of its possessor." The one who possesses wisdom-who-is-Christ has not just navigational advantage but life itself: "Whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from the LORD" (Proverbs 8:35 — Wisdom speaking). The NT identifies this Wisdom as Christ (Colossians 2:3, where all treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden in him).
13: A shadow: "Consider what God has done — who can straighten what he has made crooked?" This fundamental acknowledgment of divine sovereignty over reality — even crooked reality — anticipates both the NT's recognition of the fallen world and its proclamation that Christ will ultimately make all things new (Revelation 21:5). What no human hand can straighten, the one through whom all things were created can restore (Acts 3:21).
14: The revelation of God: "God has made both the one and the other" — good times and bad, prosperity and adversity — "so that no one can discover what the future holds." This divine hiddenness of the future is part of the creaturely condition; the NT does not promise foreknowledge but promises the presence of the one who holds the future: "I know the plans I have for you" (Jeremiah 29:11), and in Christ, the one who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8) is with his people in both the good and the bad.
15: The theme: in futile days, the righteous perish in righteousness and the wicked live long — the observed injustice that cries out for resolution. The psalms of lament make this same cry; the NT's answer is the cross and resurrection. Christ, the uniquely righteous one, perishes in his righteousness — and the resurrection is God's vindication that righteousness is not ultimately extinguished but exalted (Philippians 2:9). The final judgment will reverse every apparent injustice (Revelation 20:12–13).
16: The revelation of God: "Do not be excessively righteous — why should you ruin yourself?" Qohelet's pragmatic counsel against hyper-scrupulosity is different from the NT's call to righteousness, but both address the human tendency to seek security through moral performance. The NT's resolution is not moderate righteousness but imputed righteousness: the "righteous" one is not the person who has achieved enough but the one who trusts in Christ (Romans 4:5; Philippians 3:9).
17: The revelation of God: "Do not be excessively wicked — why die before your time?" The pragmatic boundary against self-destruction through wickedness assumes that death is the consequence of wickedness — a truth that the NT grounds in the deeper reality of sin and death as connected (Romans 6:23). Christ's death is the place where this connection is broken for those who are in him.
18: The theme: grasping both wisdom and not-releasing-the-other, "for the one who fears God will avoid both extremes" — the fear of God as the axis that holds wisdom together. The NT presents Christ as the one who "feared God" (Hebrews 5:7) perfectly, the embodiment of the wisdom that avoids every extreme, and the one who mediates the fear-of-God that is the beginning of wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:30).
19: The theme: wisdom strengthens the wise more than ten rulers — wisdom as practical strength beyond political power. Christ is the one in whom true strength resides, the one who is strong when his followers are weak (2 Corinthians 12:9–10), and in whom wisdom is not an achievement but a gift (James 1:5). Ten rulers cannot match what the Wisdom of God secures for his people.
20: Direct: "There is no righteous person on earth who always does good and never sins" — a foundational assertion that Paul quotes directly in Romans 3:10–12 as scriptural proof of universal sinfulness. Christ alone is the exception to this universal: he who knew no sin (2 Corinthians 5:21) becomes sin for us so that we might become the righteousness of God in him. This verse is one of the OT texts most directly absorbed into the NT's account of why Christ's righteousness is necessary.
21: The theme: do not pay attention to everything people say — you may hear your servant cursing you. The wisdom of selective attention to criticism, combined with self-awareness that one has done the same, is the creaturely limitation of moral consistency that Ecclesiastes 7:20 names. Christ, who is spoken against throughout his ministry and trial, hears cursing without retaliating (1 Peter 2:23) — absorbing the full weight of human criticism rather than being destroyed by it.
22: The theme: "your own heart knows that you too have cursed others many times" — the self-implicating move of Wisdom that removes any pretense of moral superiority. The NT deepens this in Matthew 7:1–5: the speck and the log; and Paul: "all have sinned" (Romans 3:23). No one can stand in moral judgment; only the one who has never cursed another — Christ — can both absorb criticism and forgive the critics.
23: The theme: "I said I will be wise — but wisdom was far from me." The failure of the self-improvement project to secure wisdom is the Ecclesiastes analogue to Paul's "wretched man that I am" in Romans 7. The wisdom that Qohelet cannot capture through effort is the wisdom the NT presents as a person — Christ — who must be received as gift (1 Corinthians 1:30) rather than achieved by resolve.
24: The theme: "whatever exists is far off and utterly deep — who can discover it?" The epistemic limit of the human knower before the depths of reality is resolved only in the NT's claim that God has revealed his deep purposes in Christ: "the deep things of God" are known through the Spirit (1 Corinthians 2:10), and in Christ "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3). What is far off and deep is made near in the Incarnation.
25: The revelation of God: Qohelet's turning of the mind to understand wisdom and the explanation of things, to know that wickedness is folly and foolishness is madness — the prophetic dimension of the investigation. Christ is the one who performs the definitive diagnosis of human wickedness and folly, not by philosophical analysis but by entering it, absorbing its consequence, and rising above it (1 Corinthians 1:18–25).
26: The theme: the woman whose heart is a trap and hands are chains — "more bitter than death" — functions in Wisdom literature as the personification of deadly folly (Proverbs 7:27; Ecclesiastes 9:18). The one who pleases God escapes her. Christ, who pleased God perfectly (Matthew 3:17), is the one who was exposed to every trap and snare of the adversary and emerged unbound — and who liberates those caught (Hebrews 2:14–15).
27: The revelation of God: "Here is what I found, says the Preacher, adding one thing to another to find the result" — the investigative process of Wisdom. The NT presents Christ as the one in whom the investigation reaches its end: not the sum of accumulated observations but the one who is himself the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6).
28: The theme: "one man in a thousand I have found, but not one woman among all these" — the failed search for a person of integrity in the context of the Wisdom investigation. The hyperbole serves the larger argument: one in a thousand is not enough; the NT's claim is that the one needed — perfectly righteous, perfectly wise — has been found in Christ (Hebrews 7:26: "holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners").
29: Direct: "God made human beings upright, but they have gone in search of many devices." This verse is the Ecclesiastes account of the Fall — in Wisdom idiom rather than Genesis narrative language, but naming the same reality: original uprightness lost to autonomous human striving. The NT's second Adam, Jesus Christ, reverses the "search for many devices" by doing only what the Father shows him (John 5:19), living the uprightness that Adam abandoned. Romans 5:18–19 is the NT commentary on this verse.
